lessons.title: What Is a Container?
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Have you ever heard a programmer say "but it works on MY computer!" when an app breaks on someone else's machine? Containers were invented to make that complaint disappear.

**Build.** A **container** packages an app together with *everything it needs to run* -- the program itself, the code libraries it depends on, configuration files, even the right versions of those pieces -- into one neat, portable bundle. Think of a real **shipping container**: whatever you pack inside, the sealed box moves the same way onto a truck, a train, or a ship. A software container does the same for an app: because the bundle carries its own dependencies, it runs the *same way* on your laptop, a teammate's PC, or a server in the cloud.

Why does "works on my machine" happen at all? Usually one computer has a slightly different version of some library or setting than another, so the app behaves differently. A container freezes all of that *inside the bundle*, so the surroundings stop mattering. The popular tools for building and running containers are **Docker** and **Podman** -- we will meet both.

**Recap.** A container is a portable bundle of an app plus everything it needs, so it runs identically anywhere -- the cure for "it works on my machine."

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a container?
choices: A portable bundle of an app plus everything it needs to run
A faster type of hard drive
A programming language for the web
A backup copy of your files
items.explanation: A container packages the app together with its libraries, files, and the right versions, so the whole thing travels as one portable unit. It is not storage hardware or a language.

items.prompt: What problem were containers mainly designed to solve?
choices: The "it works on my machine" problem, where an app behaves differently on different computers
items.explanation: By bundling the app with its dependencies, a container makes the surrounding machine stop mattering, so the app runs the same everywhere. The other options are unrelated to containers.

items.prompt: Why might an app run fine on one computer but break on another, without containers?
choices: The machines may have different versions of libraries or settings the app depends on
items.explanation: Apps depend on specific libraries and configuration; small differences between machines cause different behavior. A container locks those dependencies inside the bundle so they match everywhere.

items.prompt: The "shipping container" analogy fits software containers because a shipping container:
choices: Holds its contents in one sealed box that moves the same way anywhere
items.explanation: Just as a sealed shipping container moves identically by truck, train, or ship, a software container carries its app and dependencies so it runs the same on any machine. Color and contents are beside the point.

items.prompt: Which of these does a container typically include in its bundle?
choices: Only the app's source code, nothing else
The app plus the libraries, files, and versions it needs to run
items.explanation: The whole point is to bundle the app together with its dependencies (libraries, files, correct versions). Shipping only the source code would leave the "works on my machine" problem unsolved.

items.prompt: Which two tools are the popular ones for building and running containers?
items.explanation: Docker and Podman are the well-known container tools. The others are office, browser, or image-editing programs, not container engines.

items.prompt: If a container runs correctly on your laptop, what can you reasonably expect when you run the same container on a cloud server?
choices: It should behave the same way, because it carries its own dependencies
It must be completely rewritten first
It will only work at night
items.explanation: Carrying its dependencies is exactly what makes a container portable: the same bundle behaves the same way across machines. That predictability is the main benefit.

items.prompt: "Portable" in the context of containers most nearly means:
choices: Small enough to fit in a pocket
Able to run the same way across different computers
items.explanation: Portability here means the container runs consistently on many different machines, not anything about physical size or power. That consistency is what teams rely on.

items.prompt: A teammate cannot get your app working after lots of setup steps. How does a container help?
choices: It bundles the app with its setup so they just run it -- no fragile manual steps
items.explanation: Because the dependencies and setup are baked into the container, the teammate runs one bundle instead of reproducing many manual steps that are easy to get wrong.

lessons.title: Container vs Virtual Machine
lessons.content:
**Hook.** People often think a container is just a small virtual machine. That is the single most useful thing to get straight -- and it is wrong.

**Build.** Every computer has one **kernel**: the core part of the operating system that talks to the hardware (the CPU, memory, and disk). A **virtual machine (VM)** pretends to be a whole separate computer: it runs its *own* full operating system, with its *own* kernel, on top of the real machine. That is powerful but heavy -- like renting an entire separate kitchen just to make one sandwich. It takes lots of memory and can take a minute to "boot up."

A **container** is different. It does **not** bring its own kernel or its own operating system. Instead, all the containers on a machine **share the host's single kernel**, and the container only wraps up the app's own *userspace* -- its program and files. That makes a container much **lighter** and able to start almost **instantly**, because nothing has to boot. Compare it to a sealed **lunchbox** you carry into a kitchen that already exists, instead of renting a whole new kitchen. The trade-off: because they share one kernel, containers are isolated a little less strongly than VMs.

**Recap.** A VM carries a whole operating system and its own kernel (heavy, slow to start); a container shares the host's kernel and packages only the app (lightweight, instant). A container is *not* a VM.

items.prompt: What is the KEY difference between a container and a virtual machine?
choices: A container shares the host's single kernel; a VM runs its own full operating system with its own kernel
A container is just a VM with a different name
A VM is always faster than a container
A container can only run on Apple computers
items.explanation: This is the central idea: a VM brings an entire OS plus its own kernel, while containers share the one host kernel and wrap only the app. They are not the same thing, and VMs are generally heavier, not faster to start.

items.prompt: What is a kernel?
choices: The core part of the operating system that talks to the hardware
items.explanation: The kernel is the central piece of the OS managing CPU, memory, and devices. Containers share the host's one kernel rather than each carrying their own.

items.prompt: Why does a container usually start almost instantly while a VM can take much longer?
choices: A container has no separate operating system to boot -- it just starts the app on the already-running host kernel
items.explanation: A VM must boot a whole guest OS and kernel; a container reuses the host kernel that is already running, so there is nothing to boot -- it simply launches the app.

items.prompt: The "sealed lunchbox vs renting a whole separate kitchen" analogy maps to:
choices: Lunchbox = container (lightweight, uses the existing kitchen); separate kitchen = VM (a whole new setup)
items.explanation: A container is the lunchbox you bring into an existing kitchen (the shared kernel), while a VM is like renting an entire separate kitchen (its own full OS). That is why containers are lighter.

items.prompt: Roughly how many kernels run when you start ten containers on one Linux host?
choices: One -- the host kernel, shared by all of them
items.explanation: All containers share the single host kernel, so there is just one kernel no matter how many containers run. Ten separate kernels would describe ten VMs instead.

items.prompt: Compared with a virtual machine, a container is generally:
choices: Lighter and faster to start, because it shares the host kernel
items.explanation: Sharing the host kernel and skipping a full OS makes containers lighter on memory and near-instant to start, which is a major reason they are popular.

items.prompt: What does a container actually isolate, if not a whole operating system?
choices: Just the app's own userspace -- its program and files
items.explanation: A container wraps the app's userspace (its process and files) while sharing the kernel. It does not virtualize hardware the way a VM does, and it does provide real isolation.

items.prompt: One trade-off of containers sharing a single kernel is that:
choices: Their isolation is a bit weaker than a VM's separate-OS isolation
They must run as a VM first
items.explanation: Because everything shares one kernel, the boundary between containers is thinner than between VMs, so isolation is somewhat weaker. This is the cost of being lightweight.

items.prompt: "A container is basically a lightweight virtual machine." This statement is:
choices: A common misconception -- containers share the host kernel and don't run their own OS
items.explanation: It is the classic beginner misconception. The defining difference is that containers share the host kernel and package only the app, rather than running a full guest OS like a VM.

lessons.title: Images vs Containers (and Registries)
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A recipe is not the same as a cooked meal. Keeping that difference in mind makes containers click.

**Build.** An **image** is the saved, read-only package -- the *recipe* or the app *installer*. It sits on disk and does nothing by itself. A **container** is what you get when you *run* an image: the recipe actually being cooked, a live program doing work. The relationship is one-way and reusable: from a single image you can start **many** containers, just as one recipe can cook many identical meals.

Where do images come from? A **registry** is an online library of ready-made images. The most famous is **Docker Hub**. You **pull** (download) an image such as `nginx` (a popular web server) from a registry, then **run** it to create a container. Each image has a **tag** that picks a version, written `name:tag`, for example `nginx:1.27`.

```bash
docker pull nginx:1.27       # download the image from a registry
docker run nginx:1.27        # start a container from that image
docker ps                    # list the containers currently running
```

**Recap.** An image is the saved template (a recipe); a container is that image running (the meal). You pull images from a registry by `name:tag`, and one image can spawn many containers.

items.prompt: What is the difference between an image and a container?
choices: An image is the saved, read-only template; a container is that image actually running
They are two words for the same thing
An image runs; a container just sits on disk
A container is always smaller than an image
items.explanation: The image is the static package (the recipe); running it produces a container (the meal being cooked). The image does nothing on its own -- the container is the live process.

items.prompt: Using the cooking analogy, an image is like the ___ and a container is like the ___.
items.explanation: An image is the recipe (a saved set of instructions and ingredients); a container is the meal you cook from it. One recipe can make many meals, just as one image starts many containers.

items.prompt: What is a registry?
choices: A list of running containers on your machine
An online library you pull ready-made images from
items.explanation: A registry (such as Docker Hub) stores images that anyone can pull. It is not a process list -- that is what `docker ps` shows -- nor part of the kernel.

items.prompt: In the image name `nginx:1.27`, what is the `1.27` part?
choices: A tag that selects a specific version of the image
The amount of memory it uses
items.explanation: After the colon comes the tag, which picks a particular version of the image. It has nothing to do with container limits, price, or memory.

items.prompt: How many containers can you start from a single image?
choices: Many -- one image can spawn many independent containers
Only as many as the image's version number
items.explanation: Images are reusable templates, so one image can launch many separate containers, like cooking many meals from one recipe. They certainly can be run -- that is their purpose.

items.prompt: What does `docker pull nginx:1.27` do?
choices: Downloads the nginx image (version 1.27) from a registry
items.explanation: `pull` downloads the named image and tag from a registry to your machine. Running it later (`docker run`) is the separate step that creates a container.

items.prompt: Why is an image described as "read-only"?
choices: You can only view it on a screen
It is a fixed template that isn't changed when you run it; the running container holds the changes
items.explanation: The image stays unchanged as a stable template; any changes happen in the running container, not the image. That is what lets one image reliably produce many identical containers.

items.prompt: Which command lists the containers currently running?
items.explanation: `docker ps` shows running containers. The others are not real container-listing commands.

items.prompt: What is the usual order of steps to get a web server running from a registry?
choices: Run the container, then invent an image
Pull the image from the registry, then run it to start a container
Delete an image, then pull it
Start a VM, then a kitchen
items.explanation: First you pull (download) the image, then you run it to create a container. You cannot run an image you have not obtained, and you do not need a VM for this.

lessons.title: Using Containers: docker run, Ports & Volumes
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You have an image. How do you actually start it, reach it from your web browser, and keep its data from vanishing? Three small ideas: run, ports, and volumes.

**Build.** The workhorse command is **`docker run`**. Give it an image name and it starts a container from that image. Handy flags: `-d` runs it in the background ("detached"), and `--name` gives it a friendly name.

A container is isolated, so by default the outside world cannot reach a server inside it. **Port mapping** opens a door. The flag `-p 8080:80` means "connect **host** port 8080 to **container** port 80" (the order is always *host:container*). Now visiting `localhost:8080` in your browser reaches the app listening on port 80 inside.

When a container is deleted, anything it wrote inside disappears. To keep data, use a **volume**: storage that lives *outside* the container and is mounted into it. The flag `-v mydata:/data` attaches a volume named `mydata` to the folder `/data` inside, so the files survive even if you delete and recreate the container.

```bash
docker run -d --name web -p 8080:80 -v mydata:/data nginx
docker logs web      # see the container's output
docker stop web      # stop it when finished
```

**Recap.** `docker run` starts a container; `-p host:container` maps a port so you can reach the app; `-v` attaches a volume so data outlives the container.

items.prompt: What does `docker run nginx` do?
choices: Starts a new container from the nginx image
items.explanation: `docker run` launches a container from the named image. Listing containers is `docker ps`, and running does not delete or edit anything.

items.prompt: In `docker run -p 8080:80 nginx`, what does `-p 8080:80` do?
choices: Maps host port 8080 to container port 80
Maps container port 8080 to host port 80
Limits the container to 80 MB of memory
items.explanation: The format is host:container, so traffic to the host's port 8080 is forwarded to port 80 inside the container. It is not a range, and it has nothing to do with memory.

items.prompt: Why do you usually need port mapping to reach a web app inside a container?
choices: Containers cannot use networks at all
A container is isolated, so by default the outside world can't reach a server inside it
The browser is too slow otherwise
items.explanation: Isolation means the container's ports aren't exposed by default; `-p` opens a door from a host port to a container port. Containers can use networks -- mapping just connects them to the host.

items.prompt: What is a volume used for?
choices: Keeping data that should survive even if the container is deleted
items.explanation: A volume is storage outside the container, mounted in, so data persists across container deletion and recreation. Version selection is done with a tag, not a volume.

items.prompt: By default, what happens to files a container wrote inside itself when you delete that container?
choices: They disappear along with the container
They become a new image automatically
items.explanation: Without a volume, in-container changes vanish when the container is removed. That is exactly why volumes exist -- to keep important data outside the container.

items.prompt: What does the `-d` flag in `docker run -d nginx` do?
choices: Deletes the container after one second
Runs the container in the background (detached)
items.explanation: `-d` runs the container detached, in the background, so your terminal is free. It does not delete the container or disable networking.

items.prompt: You ran `docker run -p 3000:80 nginx`. Which browser address reaches the app?
items.explanation: The host side of the mapping is 3000, so you connect to localhost:3000, and traffic is forwarded to port 80 inside. The host number is the one you type in the browser.

items.prompt: In `-v mydata:/data`, what does `/data` refer to?
choices: The folder inside the container where the volume is attached
items.explanation: `/data` is the path inside the container where the named volume `mydata` is mounted, so writes there land in the persistent volume rather than the throwaway container.

items.prompt: Which command shows the output (logs) a running container has produced?
items.explanation: `docker logs <name>` prints the container's output, which is the usual way to see what it is doing. `docker pull` downloads images; the others are not real commands.

lessons.title: Writing a Dockerfile: FROM, RUN, COPY, CMD & Layers
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Where do images come from in the first place? You write a short recipe file called a **Dockerfile**, and Docker bakes it into an image.

**Build.** A Dockerfile is a plain text file of instructions, read top to bottom. The main ones:

- **`FROM`** -- the starting point, a *base image* to build on (e.g. `FROM python:3.12` gives you Python already installed).
- **`COPY`** -- copy files from your project into the image (e.g. your code).
- **`RUN`** -- run a command while building, such as installing packages. Its result is baked into the image.
- **`CMD`** -- the default command to run when a container starts. (`ENTRYPOINT` is a close cousin that sets the fixed program to run; `CMD` often supplies its default arguments.)

Each instruction adds a **layer** -- a stacked slice of the image. Docker is smart: it **caches** layers and reuses any whose inputs have not changed (the **build cache**). So **order matters**: copy your dependency list and install packages *before* copying your source code. Then editing your code only rebuilds the small last layer, not the slow "install everything" layer.

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.12
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "app.py"]
```

Build it with `docker build -t myapp .` (the `-t` names the image; the `.` is the folder to build from).

**Recap.** A Dockerfile lists steps -- `FROM`, `COPY`, `RUN`, `CMD` -- and each step adds a cached layer. Put slow, rarely-changing steps first so the build cache saves you time.

items.prompt: What is a Dockerfile?
choices: A text file of instructions used to build an image
items.explanation: A Dockerfile is the recipe: a list of build steps Docker follows to bake an image. It is not the running container or the registry.

items.prompt: What does the `FROM` instruction do?
choices: Sets the base image to build on top of
items.explanation: `FROM` chooses the starting base image (for example `python:3.12`), giving you a foundation to add to. Port mapping and running the app are separate concerns.

items.prompt: Which instruction copies files from your project into the image?
items.explanation: `COPY` brings files (like your source code) from the build folder into the image. `FROM` sets the base, `CMD` sets the default run command, and `STOP` is not a Dockerfile instruction.

items.prompt: What does `RUN` do in a Dockerfile?
choices: Runs a command during the build (e.g. installing packages), baking the result into the image
Pulls an image from a registry
items.explanation: `RUN` executes a command at build time and saves the result as a layer. The command that runs when a container later starts is set by `CMD`/`ENTRYPOINT`, not `RUN`.

items.prompt: What does `CMD` specify?
choices: The default command to run when a container starts
items.explanation: `CMD` sets the default command executed when the container launches (e.g. `python app.py`). The base image is `FROM`, and ports are handled at run time with `-p`.

items.prompt: What is a layer in a container image?
choices: A stacked slice added by an instruction, which Docker can cache and reuse
items.explanation: Each instruction adds a layer; Docker caches layers and reuses unchanged ones to speed up rebuilds. Layers are a software concept, not hardware or networking.

items.prompt: Why copy your dependency list and install packages BEFORE copying your source code?
choices: So editing your code doesn't bust the cache for the slow package-install layer
It is required or the build fails
items.explanation: Docker reuses cached layers whose inputs are unchanged. If code is copied first, every code edit invalidates the later install layer; installing deps first keeps that expensive layer cached across code changes.

items.prompt: What does `docker build -t myapp .` do?
choices: Builds an image named myapp from the Dockerfile in the current folder
items.explanation: `docker build` reads the Dockerfile and produces an image; `-t myapp` names it and `.` is the build folder. Running, deleting, and pushing are different commands.

items.prompt: How is the relationship between ENTRYPOINT and CMD best summarized for a beginner?
choices: They do the exact same thing and cannot be combined
ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed program to run; CMD often supplies its default arguments
CMD sets the base image; ENTRYPOINT maps ports
Neither has anything to do with starting the container
items.explanation: A common pattern is ENTRYPOINT = the program that always runs, with CMD providing default arguments that a user can override at run time. Both relate to what runs when the container starts.

items.prompt: The "build cache" mainly helps by:
choices: Reusing unchanged layers so rebuilds are faster
items.explanation: The build cache reuses layers whose inputs haven't changed, so you don't redo slow steps each build. It is purely a speed feature, not about security or ports.

lessons.title: Under the Hood: Namespaces & cgroups
lessons.content:
**Hook.** A container felt almost magical -- a lightweight box around an app. The magic is really just two features built into the Linux kernel.

**Build.** Remember that a container shares the host kernel and is just an ordinary process. So how does that process get its own private "world" and stay within limits? Two kernel tools, working together.

**Namespaces** provide **isolation** -- they control what a process can *see*. Linux can give a process its own private view of several things: its own list of running processes, its own filesystem, its own network, its own hostname. Inside its namespaces, the app looks like it has the machine to itself, even though other containers are running right beside it. (Picture office cubicles: everyone shares one building, but each person sees only their own desk.)

**cgroups** ("control groups") provide **limits** -- they control how much a process can *use*. A cgroup can cap CPU time and set a memory ceiling, so one container cannot hog the whole machine. This is what enforces a flag like `docker run -m 256m` (limit to 256 MB of memory).

So the one-line truth: a container is just a normal process wrapped by **namespaces (what it can see)** and **cgroups (how much it can use)** -- all on the shared host kernel. No second operating system is involved.

**Recap.** Namespaces = isolation (a private view); cgroups = resource limits (a usage cap). Together they turn an ordinary process into a container.

items.prompt: What do namespaces provide for a container?
choices: Isolation -- a private view of things like processes, files, and the network
items.explanation: Namespaces isolate what a process can see, giving it its own view of processes, filesystem, network, and hostname. Capping resources is the job of cgroups, not namespaces.

items.prompt: What do cgroups (control groups) provide?
choices: Resource limits -- caps on how much CPU and memory a process may use
items.explanation: cgroups limit and account resource usage (CPU, memory, and more), so one container cannot starve the others. Providing a private view is what namespaces do instead.

items.prompt: The simplest summary of a container is:
choices: A small virtual machine with its own kernel
An ordinary process wrapped by namespaces (isolation) and cgroups (limits) on the shared host kernel
items.explanation: That one-liner captures it: a normal process plus namespaces for isolation and cgroups for limits, all on the host kernel. It is explicitly not a VM with its own kernel.

items.prompt: "Isolation" in a container most nearly means:
choices: The process gets its own private view, as if it had the machine to itself
items.explanation: Isolation here means the process sees its own processes, files, and network, unaware of its neighbors. That private view is exactly what namespaces create.

items.prompt: Which kernel feature enforces a memory cap like `docker run -m 256m`?
items.explanation: Resource limits are a cgroups feature: a cgroup sets the memory ceiling and the kernel enforces it. Namespaces only change what is visible, not how much can be used.

items.prompt: The "office cubicles in one building" analogy illustrates which idea?
choices: Namespaces giving each process its own private view while sharing one machine
items.explanation: Everyone shares one building (the host kernel) but sees only their own desk -- that is namespace isolation. Limiting how much each person can use would be the cgroups idea.

items.prompt: Do namespaces and cgroups require the container to run a second operating system?
choices: Yes, each container boots its own OS
No -- they are features of the one shared host kernel; no second OS is involved
items.explanation: Both are built into the single host kernel that all containers share, which is why containers stay lightweight. Running a second OS would make it a VM, not a container.

items.prompt: If you wanted to stop one container from using all the host's CPU, which primitive handles that?
items.explanation: cgroups cap resource usage such as CPU time, preventing one container from monopolizing the machine. Namespaces and port mapping deal with visibility and networking, not usage caps.

items.prompt: Two containers run side by side. Why does each one seem to have its own separate set of running processes?
choices: Because each has its own kernel
Because a namespace gives each its own private process view
Because they take turns every second
items.explanation: A process (PID) namespace gives each container its own view of running processes. They still share one kernel, and it is namespaces -- not cgroups -- that control visibility.

lessons.title: Podman, Best Practices & a Glimpse of Kubernetes
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Docker is the most famous container tool, but it is not the only one -- and a few simple habits make your containers much safer.

**Build.** **Podman** is an alternative to Docker that uses almost the same commands, so much that `alias docker=podman` often just works. The big difference is its design. Docker runs a single always-on background service called a **daemon** (`dockerd`) that runs as the powerful **root** user and owns every container. Podman is **daemonless**: there is no central background service -- it simply starts each container as a direct child of your command. This makes Podman naturally **rootless**: an ordinary user can run containers without root powers, and "root" *inside* the container maps to your ordinary unprivileged user *outside*. If a rootless container is broken into, the attacker only gets your limited account, not the whole machine.

```bash
podman run -d -p 8080:80 nginx     # same idea as docker, no daemon needed
```

A few **best practices** worth knowing:

- **Use a small base image** (like a slim or minimal image). Less software inside means fewer security holes and a smaller download.
- **Don't run as root inside the container.** Add a non-root user so a break-in does less damage.

**The horizon -- Kubernetes.** When you need to run many containers across many machines, automatically restarting and scaling them, teams use **Kubernetes** -- a system that orchestrates containers across a whole cluster. You do not need it to start out; just know it exists as the next step up.

**Recap.** Podman is a daemonless, rootless alternative to Docker with the same commands. Prefer small base images and a non-root user. Kubernetes is where containers go to scale across many machines.

items.prompt: What is the main architectural difference between Podman and Docker?
choices: Podman can only run one container
Podman is daemonless -- it starts each container directly, with no always-on root background service
Podman uses completely different commands you must relearn
items.explanation: Docker relies on a long-running root daemon; Podman skips it and launches containers as direct child processes. Its commands are intentionally Docker-compatible, and it supports ports normally.

items.prompt: What does "rootless" mean for a container?
choices: An ordinary, unprivileged user can run it; root inside maps to that limited user outside
It runs without any user account
items.explanation: Rootless means a normal user runs containers without host root powers; in-container root maps to their unprivileged account. It still has files and can use the network.

items.prompt: Why is rootless generally safer?
choices: If a container is broken into, the attacker only gets the limited user account, not the whole machine
items.explanation: Without host root, a container escape yields only the unprivileged user's access rather than full control of the host. It is about limiting damage, not speed or magic bug prevention.

items.prompt: What is a daemon, in the Docker sense?
choices: A long-running background service that manages containers
items.explanation: Docker's daemon (`dockerd`) is an always-on background service, running as root, that owns and manages containers. Podman avoids having one. It is not an image or a cable.

items.prompt: Why prefer a small (minimal) base image?
choices: Less software inside means fewer security holes and a smaller download
It forces the container to run as root
items.explanation: A minimal base ships fewer packages, shrinking both the attack surface (fewer vulnerabilities) and the image size. It does not change the user or the kernel features used.

items.prompt: Why is running as a non-root user inside a container a best practice?
choices: If the container is compromised, a non-root process limits the damage an attacker can do
It is required for the image to build
items.explanation: Least privilege: a compromised root process inside a container is far more dangerous than an unprivileged one, so adding a non-root user reduces risk. It is not about speed or a build requirement.

items.prompt: What is Kubernetes, in one line?
choices: A system that runs and orchestrates many containers across many machines, restarting and scaling them
A replacement for the Linux kernel
items.explanation: Kubernetes orchestrates containers across a cluster, handling scheduling, restarts, and scaling. It is not an editor, a kernel, or an image registry.

items.prompt: When would a team typically reach for Kubernetes?
choices: To start a single container on one laptop
When they need to run and scale many containers across many machines automatically
To pull one image from a registry
items.explanation: Kubernetes shines at scale -- many containers across many nodes with automatic restarts and scaling. For a single container on one machine, plain Docker or Podman is enough.

items.prompt: Roughly how different are everyday Podman commands from Docker commands?
choices: Very similar -- often `alias docker=podman` just works
Podman has no commands at all
Podman only accepts commands in binary
items.explanation: Podman deliberately mirrors Docker's command-line interface, so most commands are interchangeable and an alias commonly suffices. You do not have to relearn everything.

items.prompt: You start one small web service on a single server. Do you need Kubernetes?
choices: No -- plain Docker or Podman is plenty; Kubernetes is for scaling across many machines
Only if you use a volume
Only if you write a Dockerfile
items.explanation: A single service on one machine runs fine with Docker or Podman alone. Kubernetes adds value when you must orchestrate many containers across a cluster, which is overkill here.
